nameMeanings.name: Beaufou
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: Norman, English: locative name in the case of the Domesday Book tenants from Beaufour (Calvados), which is recorded as Belfou, Beaufou in 1100 and as Bellefai in about 1160 ( Tengvik ).

variants.name: Domesday Book
meanings: English: locative name from either of two places in Lancs, namely Bispham near Blackpool ( Bischopham, 1086 in Domesday Book ) or Bispham in Croston.

variants.name: Tengvik
meanings: 1 English: relationship name from the Middle English personal name Bat(te), probably a pet form of Middle English Bartelmew ( Bartholomew) or perhaps the rarer Bartram ( Bertram). Compare Bett and Bate , with which Bat(te) may often have been interchangeable; and compare the pet form Bateman , also found as Batman and Battman.

2 English: (i) nickname probably in many cases a variant of Back ‘bat’ (the winged animal). The alteration of Middle English bakke to bat is not on record before 1580 in OED but the frequency with which le Bat occurs in medieval documents suggests that this is a likely source of the name. (ii) nickname alternatively from Middle English bat(te) (Old English batt) ‘cudgel, club, bat’, perhaps for someone of stout appearance or for one who wielded a bat. Compare Bate (1) and Ælfricus qui Bata cognominabatur, about 1051 in Tengvik . Examples below without the definite article may belong under (2) or (3).

nameMeanings.name: Emma
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Old French name, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, originally a short form of compound names such as Ermintrude containing the word erm(en), irm(en) ‘entire’. It was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to Britain, but its popularity in medieval England was greatly enhanced by the fact that it had been borne by the mother of Edward the Confessor, herself a Norman. In modern times, it was only in moderate use early in the 20th century but rose sharply in favour in the 1970s and has since remained perennially popular.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

variants.name: Deborah
meanings: Biblical name (meaning ‘bee’ in Hebrew), borne by the nurse of Rebecca (Genesis 35:8) and by a woman judge and prophet (Judges 4–5) who led the Israelites to victory over the Canaanites. It has always been popular as a Jewish name. It was in use among Christians by the mid 16th century and was taken up by the Puritans in the 17th century, in part because the bee was a symbol of industriousness. Since then it has enjoyed enormous popularity, peaking in the 1960s. Among other famous bearers is the actress  Deborah Kerr  (  1921–2007 ).

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

historicEvents.title: France Declares War on England
historicEvents.description: France declared war on England as Louis VIII of France attacked Poitou and northern Gascony.

en.lifeSummary: When Emma de Beaufou was born about 1194, in Hockwold cum Wilton, Norfolk, England, her father, Fulk de Beaufou, was 26 and her mother, Agatha, was 24. She had at least 1 daughter with Gilbert De Norfolk. She died after 1239, in King's Lynn, Norfolk, England, United Kingdom.

Elle a eu au moins 1 fille avec Gilbert De Norfolk. Elle est décédée après 1239, à King's Lynn, Norfolk, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

ko.lifeSummary: Emma de Beaufou(이)가 대략 1194년 영국 노르폴크 호ck울드 c 윌턴에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Fulk de Beaufou(은)는 26세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Agatha(은)는 24세였다. 그녀는 Gilbert De Norfolk(와)과 적어도 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1239년 이후 영국 잉글랜드 노르폴크 킨그'스 린에서 사망했다.
